checks.a_analysis: The output sections accurately reflect the text found on the non-omitted pages (1, 2, 3, 4, 24, 25). The titles and prompt text in the tables match the source exactly. No instances of hallucinated or invented text were found in the provided segments.

The only contact information provided in the JSON is the name 'Josh Meredith' and his title/division, which matches Page 2 of the source. The email address present in the source is omitted rather than fabricated. No incorrect URLs, phone numbers, or emails were introduced.

The output contains a significant omission. Pages 5 through 23 of the source document, which include an annotated prompt example, detailed usage instructions for the document and chat pane, and several practice exercises, are entirely missing. The JSON output explicitly confirms this with the entry '"_omitted": "19 sections omitted for token budget"'.
checks.b_evidence_source: Pages 5 through 23 of the original 25-page document, including slides such as 'Practice : Create a Copilot training plan' (p8), 'Practice : Visualize Content as a Table' (p18), and 'Practice : Summarize a Document' (p20).
checks.b_evidence_location: The omission occurs between the table for Page 4 (reading_order 21) and the table for Page 24 (reading_order 158).

checks.a_analysis: The tables generated for Page 4 and Page 24 were examined. The Page 4 table correctly maps the 4 categories (Goal, Context, Source, Expectations). The Page 24 table captures all 12 prompt tiles shown in the source PDF. No rows are missing from these tables.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: Within the sections that were included, the text fidelity is high. On Page 2, the email address 'Josh.Meredith@microsoft.com' is omitted, representing one line out of five (20%), which is below the 25% threshold for a MAJOR defect. The major omission of pages is captured under C3.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The text in the provided sections matches the source PDF. Minor OCR discrepancies (such as '3key' in the Page 24 table vs '3 key' in the PDF image) are consistent with the provided OCR text and are considered acceptable punctuation/spacing variations.

checks.a_analysis: I have reviewed the entire output and compared it against the source PDF. Each text block and slide content appears exactly once in the output, mirroring its single occurrence in the source. There is no evidence of repeated paragraphs, sections, or list items.

The output consistently uses H1 tags for slide titles (e.g., 'Copilot in Word', 'Topics', 'Art of Prompting'). This is a standard and appropriate normalization for a slide-based document, ensuring each major topic is easily navigable for screen readers. No heading levels are skipped.

The document contains two complex grid layouts (on Page 4 and Page 24) that have been converted into tables. On Page 4, the four-column grid is correctly structured with a header row for categories and a data row for details. On Page 24, the 12-item grid is represented as a four-column table where titles and descriptions are logically grouped in alternating rows. These structures effectively preserve the visual and semantic relationships found in the source PDF.

The information flow in the output follows the logical progression of the slides from the source PDF. The title slide is followed by the presenter bio, the table of contents (Topics), and then the substantive content slides. Within each slide, the elements (headings, text, tables, images) are ordered correctly, reflecting the intended consumption path.
The bulleted list on the 'Topics' slide (Page 3) is correctly identified and converted into an unordered list in the output JSON. The list items match the source exactly in content and sequence.

The output contains no link elements. While the source PDF contains a text-based email address on Page 2, it is not formatted as a hyperlink in the semantic output, which is acceptable. No broken, missing, or fabricated links were detected.

checks.a_analysis: The conversion from a 25-page slide deck into a single-column HTML document is executed logically. The multi-column layouts of the slides (such as the biography on page 2 and the 'Art of Prompting' elements on page 4) are linearized appropriately for a responsive HTML format, maintaining the original reading order and hierarchy. The visual organization of information matches the source's intent.

The grid of 12 prompt cards found on page 24 of the source PDF is rendered as a clean 4-column table in the HTML. The table structure is visually intact, with clear cell distinctions and legible content. While there is extra metadata text present (addressed in V4), the table itself is not broken and the data is understandable.

All meaningful images from the source PDF are present in the HTML output. This includes the Microsoft and Copilot logos, the headshot of Josh Meredith, the three supplemental photos on page 2, the various icons on the prompting slides, and the numerous UI screenshots used for instructional purposes.

The HTML output exhibits significant visual duplication of a metadata string. The text 'Microsoft Word Logo - PNG and Vector - Logo Download' is rendered as visible body text on page 6, page 7, and most notably repeated 12 times within the table on page 24 (once in every cell). This text is not visible in the source PDF and constitutes 'repeated body content beyond what the source shows,' cluttering the visual flow and indicating a conversion error where image alt-text or metadata has been exposed and duplicated across multiple instances of an icon.
checks.b_evidence_source: Visual grid of 12 prompt cards on Page 24 contains only headings, text, and icons; no 'Microsoft Word Logo...' text is visible.
checks.b_evidence_output: "Microsoft Word Logo - PNG and Vector - Logo Download" (repeated 12 times in the table on page 24, and on pages 6 and 7).
checks.b_evidence_location: HTML output corresponding to Page 24, 6, and 7
